expanded_description: The special quadrilaterals—parallelograms, rectangles, rhombuses, squares, trapezoids, and kites—each have defining properties that can be proved using triangle congruence, parallel line theorems, and coordinate methods. This section focuses on constructing formal proofs that establish a quadrilateral is a specific type based on given information, and proofs that derive additional properties from a known classification.

core_concept.principle: Proofs for special parallelograms follow a two-stage strategy: first prove the quadrilateral is a parallelogram, then prove the additional property that makes it a rectangle, rhombus, or square. For trapezoids and kites, independent proof paths are used. Triangle congruence, established by drawing a diagonal, is the primary proof tool.
core_concept.explanation: Quadrilateral proofs are like building with blocks. The parallelogram is the foundational block. Stack on 'one right angle' and you get a rectangle. Stack on 'consecutive congruent sides' and you get a rhombus. Stack on both and you get a square. The trapezoid and kite are separate starting blocks with their own add-ons. In a proof, you first establish which block you have, then you can use all its properties. Drawing a diagonal creates two triangles, and triangle congruence becomes your primary tool. CPCTC then gives you the sides and angles you need.

image.alt: Building-block diagram: Quadrilateral → Parallelogram → Rectangle/Rhombus → Square. Separate branches for Trapezoid and Kite. Each level lists properties gained.
image.caption: Proof strategy: parallelogram first, then special property. Triangle congruence via a diagonal.

sections.section_title: Proving a Quadrilateral Is a Special Type
sections.introduction: The path to proving a quadrilateral is a rectangle, rhombus, or square has two stages: prove it is a parallelogram, then prove the additional distinguishing property. Trapezoids and kites have their own independent proof paths.

introduction_image.alt: Two-stage proof template. Stage 1: five conditions for parallelogram. Stage 2: specific conditions for rectangle, rhombus, square.
introduction_image.caption: Two-stage proof: parallelogram first, then the special property.

key_concept.definition: To prove a rectangle: prove parallelogram, then one right angle OR congruent diagonals. To prove a rhombus: prove parallelogram, then consecutive congruent sides OR perpendicular diagonals. To prove a square: prove both rectangle and rhombus conditions. To prove an isosceles trapezoid: prove trapezoid (one pair parallel sides), then congruent legs OR congruent base angles OR congruent diagonals. To prove a kite: prove two pairs of adjacent congruent sides.
key_concept.context: These proof paths use converses of the properties from Sections 6.2–6.7. The two-stage structure works because rectangles, rhombuses, and squares are defined as special parallelograms.

image.alt: Flowchart showing proof paths for each quadrilateral type from given information.
image.caption: Proof paths for each special quadrilateral.

process_flow.description: Identify which quadrilateral type the given information points toward.
process_flow.mechanism: Congruent opposite sides → parallelogram path. One right angle → rectangle path. Congruent consecutive sides → rhombus path. Perpendicular diagonals → rhombus path (if parallelogram). Congruent diagonals → rectangle path (if parallelogram). One pair parallel sides → trapezoid path.
applications: 'Diagonals bisect each other and are congruent' → rectangle.

image.alt: Decision tree from given information to target quadrilateral type.
image.caption: Step 1: Determine the target quadrilateral from the given information.

process_flow.description: Unless already given or a trapezoid/kite, establish parallelogram status using one of the five conditions.
process_flow.mechanism: Draw a diagonal. Use SSS, SAS, ASA, or AAS to prove the two triangles congruent. CPCTC gives parallel or congruent opposite sides. Alternatively, use given diagonal bisection or angle congruence.
applications: 'AB ≅ CD, AD ≅ BC' → SSS → △ABC ≅ △CDA → CPCTC → both pairs parallel → parallelogram.

image.alt: Two-column proof establishing parallelogram via triangle congruence.
image.caption: Step 2: Prove the quadrilateral is a parallelogram.

process_flow.description: Add the distinguishing condition to reach the final classification.
process_flow.mechanism: Rectangle: one angle 90° (perpendicular adjacent sides) or congruent diagonals. Rhombus: consecutive sides congruent or perpendicular diagonals. Square: both conditions. Use CPCTC from the triangle congruence established in Step 2.
applications: Parallelogram + AB ≅ BC → all four sides congruent → rhombus.
Parallelogram + diagonals perpendicular → four congruent right triangles → rhombus.

image.alt: The special property added to the parallelogram, completing the proof.
image.caption: Step 3: Prove the special property to finalize classification.

image.alt: Complete section overview with two-stage proof strategy and decision flowchart.
image.caption: Proofs with Special Quadrilaterals overview.

sections.introduction: Apply the proof strategies to establish quadrilateral classifications and derive properties.

worked_examples.title: Special Quadrilateral Proofs
worked_examples.problem:
1) Given: Parallelogram ABCD with AC ≅ BD. Prove: Rectangle.
2) Given: Parallelogram ABCD with AB ≅ BC. Prove: Rhombus.
3) Given: Parallelogram ABCD with perpendicular diagonals. Prove: Rhombus.
4) Given: Rhombus ABCD with AC ≅ BD. Prove: Square.
5) Given: Trapezoid ABCD with AB ∥ CD and ∠A ≅ ∠B. Prove: Isosceles trapezoid.

step_by_step.explanation: Parallelogram → opposite sides ≅. AC ≅ BD (Given). SSS → △ADC ≅ △BCD. CPCTC → ∠ADC ≅ ∠BCD. Consecutive interior angles supplementary along DC, and they are congruent → each 90°. Parallelogram with one right angle → rectangle.
Parallelogram → opposite sides ≅. AB ≅ BC (Given). Transitive → all four sides ≅. Rhombus.
Parallelogram → diagonals bisect. Diagonals perpendicular. HL → four right triangles at intersection are congruent. CPCTC → all four sides ≅. Rhombus.
Rhombus → all sides ≅. AC ≅ BD → congruent diagonals. Parallelogram with congruent diagonals → rectangle. Both rhombus and rectangle → square.
Draw altitudes from D and C to AB. Right triangles formed. ∠A ≅ ∠B (Given). Altitudes equal. AAS → triangles congruent. CPCTC → AD ≅ BC. Trapezoid with congruent legs → isosceles trapezoid.

image.alt: Five panels with diagrams and proofs.
image.caption: Special quadrilateral proofs.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Two-stage proof strategy: parallelogram first, then special property. Triangle congruence as primary tool.

summary: Proving special quadrilaterals uses a two-stage strategy for parallelograms: prove parallelogram, then add the special property (right angle/congruent diagonals for rectangle; consecutive congruent sides/perpendicular diagonals for rhombus; both for square). Trapezoids and kites have independent paths. Drawing a diagonal for triangle congruence is the primary proof tool.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with proof strategies for each quadrilateral type.
review_questions_image.caption: Proofs with Special Quadrilaterals Review.
